Multidrug-resistant Escherichia coli and Salmonella spp. in quail eggs from Lima, Peru: Local surveillance and global meta-analysis of prevalence
abstract: Lima, a global gastronomic destination, faces biosafety risks due to the presence of multidrug-resistant (MDR) pathogens in its food supply. Quail (Coturnix coturnix japonica) eggs are highly nutritious and versatile. However, local surveillance and global evidence on their microbiological safety remain scarce and fragmented. To address these gaps, this study investigated the occurrence and antimicrobial resistance of Salmonella spp. and Escherichia coli in quail eggs sold in Lima, integrating findings into a global systematic review and meta-analysis, following PRISMA guidelines. For the local study, 125 eggs were collected via cluster sampling (n = 25 distributors), while the meta-analysis synthesized data from 15 international studies (2000-2025) systematically retrieved from five databases (Web of Science, PubMed, Scopus, EBSCO, and Google Scholar). Prevalence and 95% Credible Intervals (CrI) were estimated through Bayesian inference for the local data, and a random-effects model was used for the global synthesis. Salmonella spp. was absent locally (95% CrI: 0-1.52), consistent with the low global pooled prevalence of 2.09% (95% CI: 0.00-7.75), although high heterogeneity was observed (I= 96.6%, p < 0.0001). Conversely, E. coli was isolated from 1.6% (95% CrI: 0.33-5.03) of local eggs, sitting below the global pooled average of 10.38% (95% CI: 2.34-22.63; I= 94%, p < 0.0001), with a local distributor-level prevalence of 8.0% (95% CrI: 1.70-23.27). All isolates (3/3) were MDR, showing resistance to neomycin, erythromycin, clindamycin, cefotaxime, and ciprofloxacin. Quail eggs may serve as reservoirs for dangerous strains within traditional and global supply chains.

title: Potential Role of Brown Macroalgae on Pig Health: A Review
abstract: The transition of pig farming to intensive production, along with the shortening of the weaning age to 21-28 days, has led to increased use of in-feed antibiotic growth promoters (AGP). Following the ban on AGP in 2006 and the pharmacological level (2500 ppm) of zinc oxide in 2022 in Europe, the need for natural alternatives in pig production during weaning has substantially increased. Recently, brown macroalgae (BMA) have attracted considerable interest due to their diverse bioactive compounds with significant health-promoting potential. Therefore, this review emphasizes the use of BMA as a natural alternative to AGP use in pig health and production. Particularly, BMA-derived health-promoting compounds, potential uses, and limitations of using macroalgal biomass in pig health and production have been discussed.

title: Transfer of antibiotic resistance genes in ESBL-producing Escherichia coli from raw milk and dairy farm environments under heavy metal stress
abstract: This study was conducted to investigate the effects of heavy metals on the resistance levels exhibited by extended-spectrum β-lactamase (ESBL)-producing and non-ESBL-producing Escherichia coli isolated from cattle farms and raw milk. The findings indicated that ESBL-producing E. coli strains exhibited significantly higher resistance to Cu, Zn, and Cd than non-ESBL-producing strains. Furthermore, ESBL-producing strains harbored various plasmid-mediated heavy metal resistance genes. Whole-genome sequencing (WGS) results showed that ESBL-producing strains carried plasmids encoding 148 heavy metal resistance genes. In addition, resistance to certain antibiotics (e.g., chloramphenicol) in E. coli exhibited a significant negative correlation with resistance to specific heavy metals, including Mn and Cd (p < 0.05). By establishing intrageneric (E. coli-E. coli) and intergeneric (E. coli-Klebsiella pneumoniae) conjugation models, the conjugation transfer frequency was measured at varying Cuconcentrations (0.5-100 μg/L). The results indicated that low-to-moderate Cuconcentrations (1-10 μg/L) significantly enhanced intrageneric conjugative transfer frequency in E. coli (up to approximately two-fold). This effect was reversed by the reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenger thiourea, suggesting Cupromotes transfer via oxidative stress induction. In contrast, K. pneumoniae conjugation remained insensitive to Custress. Mechanistically, Cutreatment increased the cell aggregation between donor and recipient bacteria, decreased the negative surface charge, improved the membrane permeability, and modulated multiple stress-related pathways (e.g., metal efflux, membrane metabolism, and the SOS response). This study revealed that Cudrives the horizontal transmission of antibiotic resistance genes between environmental strains via ROS-dependent pathways, providing experimental evidence for understanding the coresistance mechanism between heavy metal and antibiotic resistance.

title: Advancing photodynamic inactivation: Mechanisms and application prospects of ionic liquids and deep eutectic solvents for enhancing the solubility, stability, and efficacy of edible photosensitizers
abstract: Foodborne pathogens present a continuing threat to food safety and public health. Photodynamic inactivation (PDI) technology utilizes photosensitizers to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) under specific light exposure, selectively destroying microbial structures while avoiding the risk of spreading antibiotic resistance. Although edible photosensitizers (EPS) are a green and scalable alternative to synthetic ones for use in food and medicine, they have several limitations, including low stability, inefficient delivery, and a single mechanism of action. The unique solvation environments of ionic liquids (ILs) and deep eutectic solvents (DESs) offer a promising strategy to address the limitations associated with EPS. In contrast to conventional organic solvents, ILs/DESs reduce the risk of toxic residues and comply with the safety requirements of food-grade applications. This review systematically examines the synergistic mechanisms between ILs/DESs and EPS and highlights emerging applications of ILs/DESs-EPS composite platforms. This review offers a theoretical foundation and practical engineering insights for developing environmentally friendly PDI strategies.
